title: My Top 5 Books of 2025
description: Welcome back to my annual reading reflection. As 2025 comes to a close, I am sharing the five books and honorable mentions that most shaped my thinking across history, meaning, leadership, and modern society.

title: Making a Comeback
description: Google I/O 2025 made XR feel relevant again by pairing Android XR with Gemini, turning headsets and glasses into context-aware assistants instead of another short-lived demo cycle.

title: My Top 5 Books of 2024
description: My 2024 reading list spans memoir, technology, history, and psychology, with each pick offering a different lens on leadership, learning, and how the modern world works.

title: The Spatial Computing Killer App is Finally Here
description: GPT-4o makes the case that multimodal, low-latency AI could be the first truly compelling everyday use case for AR and VR devices built around world-facing cameras.

title: A Week with Vision Pro: Insights and Impressions
description: After a week with Vision Pro, I break down what Apple got right in hardware and spatial computing, where the product still feels early, and who should actually consider buying one.

title: My Top 5 Books of 2023
description: My 2023 year-end list highlights the books that most influenced how I think about investing, technology, career development, and the future shaped by AI.

title: My Thoughts on XR: Apple vs. Meta
description: Apple and Meta are entering XR from very different positions, and that contrast reveals how ecosystem strategy, privacy, and product focus could shape the next stage of the market.

title: My Initial Thoughts on Vision Pro
description: Apple Vision Pro brings impressive hardware and renewed energy to XR, but its price, early software story, and familiar interaction model leave open questions about mainstream adoption.

title: My Top 5 Books of 2022
description: My 2022 reading recap covers the books that stood out most across entrepreneurship, meaning, relationships, metaverse thinking, and leadership.

title: The Making of Collect
description: Collect started as a response to social media fatigue, with user research, storyboarding, prototyping, and testing all focused on helping people connect without endless feed addiction.
